mission.title: Install and Configure Cluster API (CAPI)
mission.description: Cluster API (CAPI) is a Kubernetes operator that provides declarative APIs and tooling to simplify the provisioning, upgrading, and management of multiple Kubernetes clusters. It is particularly useful for platform operators looking to automate cluster lifecycle management across various infrastructure environments. Key trade-offs include the complexity of setup and the need for familiarity with Kubernetes concepts.

steps.title: Add the Cluster API Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official Cluster API Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add cluster-api https://charts.cluster-api.sigs.k8s.io

```

> This is the official repository for Cluster API charts.

steps.title: Install Cluster API CRDs
steps.description:
This step installs the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) required for Cluster API to function.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This YAML file contains all necessary CRDs.

steps.title: Install Cluster API controller
steps.description:
This step installs the Cluster API controller using Helm, which manages the lifecycle of clusters.

```bash

helm install cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

```

> Ensure you are in the correct namespace.

steps.title: Create a Cluster API custom resource
steps.description:
This step creates an example Cluster API custom resource to manage a Kubernetes cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: example-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    endpoint:
      host: example-cluster-control-plane
      port: 6443
EOF

```

> This creates a basic cluster resource.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: example-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    endpoint:
      host: example-cluster-control-plane
      port: 6443
EOF

steps.title: Verify the Cluster API installation
steps.description:
This step checks that the Cluster API components are running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cluster-api

```

> Look for the cluster-api-controller pod.
commands: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cluster-api

uninstall.title: Uninstall Cluster API
uninstall.description:
This step removes the Cluster API components and CRDs. Be aware that this will delete all managed clusters and associated resources.

```bash

helm uninstall cluster-api --namespace kube-system

kubectl delete -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

```

> This removes the Cluster API controller.
> This deletes the CRDs.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Cluster API
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your cluster resources. This step upgrades the Cluster API controller and verifies the installation.

```bash

kubectl get clusters -o json > cluster-backup.json

helm upgrade cluster-api cluster-api/cluster-api --namespace kube-system --version 1.12.4

kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cluster-api

```

> Backup current cluster resources.
> This upgrades the Cluster API controller.
> Verify that the pods are running after the upgrade.
commands: kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cluster-api

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no matches for kind "Cluster" in version "cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This error occurs when the CRDs are not installed or are outdated.

**Fix:**
Reapply the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/cluster-api/releases/download/v1.12.4/cluster-api-components.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'failed to connect to the API server'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster API controller cannot reach the Kubernetes API server.

**Fix:**
Check your network policies and ensure the controller has access to the API server.

troubleshooting.title: Pod 'cluster-api-controller' is CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually indicates a configuration issue or missing dependencies.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs using: kubectl logs -n kube-system <pod-name> and resolve any configuration issues.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'unable to recognize "": no matches for kind "Cluster"'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the Cluster CRD is not recognized, likely due to a version mismatch.

**Fix:**
Ensure you are using the correct version of the Cluster API CRDs and controller.

**Affected versions:** >=1.12.4

versionNotes.changes: Added support for Kubernetes 1.28, improved error handling in controllers.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated the v1beta1 API version for Cluster and Machine resources.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.11.x, ensure to migrate your resources to the new v1alpha4 API version.

resolution.summary: You should see the output of 'kubectl get clusters' showing your created clusters with their statuses.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: cluster.x-k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: Cluster
metadata:
  name: example-cluster
  namespace: default
spec:
  controlPlane:
    endpoint:
      host: example-cluster-control-plane
      port: 6443
EOF
kubectl get pods -n kube-system | grep cluster-api

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the required resources.
